beats.text: A multilayer perceptron stacks affine maps and pointwise nonlinearities, each layer transforming the activation from the layer before it.

visual.sub: Stacking layers, and why depth is hard

beats.text: Each layer computes a pre-activation from a weight matrix and bias, then passes it through a nonlinearity to get the activation.
In backpropagation the gradient at a layer is a product of one Jacobian per layer between it and the output.
If each layer multiplies by less than one the gradient vanishes geometrically; if more than one it explodes.
Picture a chain of amplifiers: fifty stages of gain zero point nine reduce the signal to about half a percent of its size.
Switching from saturating sigmoids to ReLU removed the worst diagonal saturation, but did not control the weight factor itself.
Before any gradient step, the random starting weights already decide whether signal survives the forward pass through depth.

visual.sub: Xavier and He: tuning the starting variance

beats.text: Pre-activation variance equals the fan-in times the weight variance times the incoming activation variance.
Standard normal weights multiply variance by the fan-in each layer, which for width 256 is a factor of 256 per layer.

visual.label: variance blow-up per layer, naive init at width 256

beats.text: Xavier initialization takes the harmonic compromise between the forward and backward fan counts to keep variance near one.
ReLU zeros half its inputs, so He initialization doubles the variance, keying off the fan-in alone.
For a 128-input ReLU layer, He sets a starting standard deviation of about 0.125, far below the naive value of one.
Good initialization controls variance at step zero, but weights move during training and each layer's input distribution drifts.
BatchNorm subtracts the mini-batch mean, divides by the batch standard deviation, then re-scales and re-shifts with learned parameters.
The learned scale and shift let the network recover any mean and variance it needs, including the identity.
Normalizing the batch {2,2,6,6} gives mean 4 and standard deviation 2, so an element of 5 maps to half a standard deviation.

visual.label: normalized value of z = 5 in batch {2,2,6,6}

beats.text: BatchNorm allows higher learning rates, reduces sensitivity to initialization, and acts as a mild regularizer.
The classic footgun is inference: with no batch, BatchNorm switches to running averages, so forgetting eval mode breaks it.

visual.sub: Live batch stats vs running averages

beats.text: Even well-initialized, batch-normalized networks hit a degradation problem, where adding layers made training accuracy worse.
A residual block adds a learned correction to its own input rather than building a fresh representation from scratch.
Differentiating the block gives the identity plus the block's Jacobian, adding a shortcut into the backward product.
Expanding the product leaves a bare identity term, so the output gradient reaches early layers undiminished.
Where a plain net shrinks the gradient geometrically, the skip keeps a clean copy alive, so depth becomes additive.
The skip is the load-bearing idea that took networks to hundreds of layers and runs through every Transformer block.

visual.label: ResNet depth made trainable by skips

beats.text: The first three fixes make a deep net trainable; regularization makes it generalize instead of memorizing the training set.
Dropout randomly zeros each activation with probability p, then rescales survivors so the expected activation is unchanged.
Each forward pass trains a different sub-network, so at test time the full network behaves like an implicit ensemble.
Weight decay adds an L2 penalty, so every weight is shrunk by a constant fraction before the data-driven update.
With adaptive optimizers, AdamW decouples the decay from the gradient step and is the modern default.
These fixes overlap and partly substitute for one another, each an engineering patch on signal that compounds through depth.

visual.sub: Overlapping fixes, not laws of nature
